title: Site Accessible Over Unencrypted HTTP
description: The site was scanned over plain HTTP. All traffic is transmitted without encryption.
riskImpact: All data transmitted between the user and server can be intercepted, read, and modified by anyone on the network path.
explanation: HTTP transmits data in plaintext, making it trivial for attackers to intercept credentials, session tokens, personal data.
fixSteps: Obtain and install an SSL/TLS certificate.
Configure your server to redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS.
Update all internal links to HTTPS.
Enable HSTS.

title: Site Served Over Unencrypted HTTP
description: The site is accessible over plain HTTP without TLS encryption.
riskImpact: All data including credentials are transmitted in plaintext.
explanation: HTTP transmits everything in cleartext. On any shared network, attackers can passively read all traffic.
fixSteps: Obtain and install a TLS certificate.
Configure HTTPS.
Set up HTTP to HTTPS redirects.
Enable HSTS.

codeExamples.label: Nginx redirect
codeExamples.code: server { listen 80; server_name example.com; return 301 https://$server_name$request_uri; }

title: Missing Expect-CT Header
description: Expect-CT header is not present. This header helps detect misissued certificates.
riskImpact: Without Expect-CT, the browser won't enforce Certificate Transparency, meaning misissued certificates could go undetected.
explanation: Expect-CT allows sites to opt-in to Certificate Transparency enforcement before it becomes mandatory. It helps detect certificates that were not logged in public CT logs.
fixSteps: Add Expect-CT header with enforce and max-age directives.
Include a report-uri for monitoring before enforcing.

title: Site accessible on both HTTP and HTTPS
description: Without HSTS or auto-redirect, the HTTP version is reachable and exploitable for SSL stripping.
riskImpact: 301 redirect HTTP→HTTPS; enable HSTS with includeSubDomains; preload
explanation: Without HSTS or auto-redirect, the HTTP version is reachable and exploitable for SSL stripping.
fixSteps: 301 redirect HTTP→HTTPS; enable HSTS with includeSubDomains; preload

codeExamples.label: Next.js (next.config.mjs)

title: HTTPS served on non-standard port
description: HTTPS endpoints served on ports other than 443 are common for internal / staging services but should never be the public-facing entry point. Mixed-port services confuse users and bypass default HSTS / preload expectations.
riskImpact: Front non-443 HTTPS with a reverse proxy on 443, or document the port in user-facing URLs.
explanation: HTTPS endpoints served on ports other than 443 are common for internal / staging services but should never be the public-facing entry point. Mixed-port services confuse users and bypass default HSTS / preload expectations.
fixSteps: Move public HTTPS endpoints to port 443 behind a reverse proxy.
Keep non-standard ports bound to internal-only listeners.
If a non-standard port must be exposed, add explicit HSTS for that hostname.

codeExamples.label: Next.js (next.config.mjs)

title: HTTP method override via X-Forwarded-Method / X-HTTP-Method-Override
description: Proxies that honor X-Forwarded-Method or X-HTTP-Method-Override allow clients to silently rewrite the HTTP method of a forwarded request. Combined with a TLS-terminating load balancer, this can bypass upstream method-based authorization.
riskImpact: Strip method-override headers at the load balancer; reject any request that overrides the originally-observed method.
explanation: Proxies that honor X-Forwarded-Method or X-HTTP-Method-Override allow clients to silently rewrite the HTTP method of a forwarded request. Combined with a TLS-terminating load balancer, this can bypass upstream method-based authorization.
fixSteps: Configure the reverse proxy to strip X-Forwarded-Method and X-HTTP-Method-Override from inbound requests.
Reject any inbound request whose body or path claims a different method than the wire-level method.
Document allowed method-override behavior in your API gateway.

codeExamples.label: Next.js (next.config.mjs)

title: HTTP/3 advertised via Alt-Svc header
description: An Alt-Svc response header advertising h3 or h3-29 indicates the server supports HTTP/3 over QUIC. The presence of Alt-Svc is informational but should be validated against your actual deployment.
riskImpact: No action required if HTTP/3 is intended; otherwise strip the header to avoid advertising an unsupported protocol.
explanation: An Alt-Svc response header advertising h3 or h3-29 indicates the server supports HTTP/3 over QUIC. The presence of Alt-Svc is informational but should be validated against your actual deployment.
fixSteps: Confirm HTTP/3 is actually deployed end-to-end (CDN, origin, certificates).
If HTTP/3 is not yet ready, disable Alt-Svc emission at the proxy.

codeExamples.label: Next.js (next.config.mjs)

title: OCSP stapling response present
description: TLS servers can staple the OCSP response for their certificate inside the TLS handshake. When present, browsers do not need to make a separate OCSP request, preserving privacy and reducing handshake latency.
riskImpact: No action required; this is informational. If stapling is missing, enable ssl_stapling on nginx / equivalent on your server.
explanation: TLS servers can staple the OCSP response for their certificate inside the TLS handshake. When present, browsers do not need to make a separate OCSP request, preserving privacy and reducing handshake latency.
fixSteps: Verify ssl_stapling on; ssl_stapling_verify on; (nginx) or equivalent.
Ensure the certificate chain includes the intermediate so stapling works.
Monitor the OCSP responder URL for outages that disable stapling.

codeExamples.label: Next.js (next.config.mjs)

title: Mixed-protocol content (HTTPS page loading HTTP resources)
description: Pages served over HTTPS that include subresources (scripts, images, iframes, XHR) loaded over plain HTTP trigger mixed-content blocking in browsers and expose the page to MITM downgrade of those subresources.
riskImpact: Upgrade every internal subresource to HTTPS and add a CSP with upgrade-insecure-requests.
explanation: Pages served over HTTPS that include subresources (scripts, images, iframes, XHR) loaded over plain HTTP trigger mixed-content blocking in browsers and expose the page to MITM downgrade of those subresources.
fixSteps: Replace http:// URLs in templates with protocol-relative or https:// URLs.
Add a Content-Security-Policy header with 'upgrade-insecure-requests'.
Audit third-party widgets and ad tags for HTTP-only assets.

codeExamples.label: Next.js (next.config.mjs)

title: Http No Redirect
description: Security check `http-no-redirect` evaluates whether the target meets the expected configuration. Auto-generated stub.
riskImpact: If exploited, this finding exposes the target to category-specific risks under ssl.
explanation: The `http-no-redirect` check verifies that the server does not expose the weakness represented by this category.
fixSteps: Investigate the evidence string for the actual offending value.
Apply the recommended configuration in the code examples below.
Verify the fix by re-running the scan and confirming the finding is gone.

title: Ocsp Stapling Enabled
description: Security check `ocsp-stapling-enabled` evaluates whether the target meets the expected configuration. Auto-generated stub.
riskImpact: If exploited, this finding exposes the target to category-specific risks under ssl.
explanation: The `ocsp-stapling-enabled` check verifies that the server does not expose the weakness represented by this category.
fixSteps: Investigate the evidence string for the actual offending value.
Apply the recommended configuration in the code examples below.
Verify the fix by re-running the scan and confirming the finding is gone.
